reading.title: Texto 1: The Paradox of Choice in the Information Age
reading.content:
In an era defined by an abundance of information and an ever-expanding array of options, one might reasonably assume that human beings have never been better positioned to make sound decisions. After all, we live in a world where a single internet search can yield thousands of results on any conceivable topic, where consumer reviews are available at the touch of a button, and where sophisticated algorithms curate personalised recommendations tailored to our individual preferences. On the surface, this seems like an unequivocal triumph of progress — a world in which ignorance has been rendered obsolete and choice has been democratised to an unprecedented degree.

Yet a growing body of research suggests that this abundance of choice is not the unadulterated blessing it appears to be. The psychologist Barry Schwartz, in his influential work *The Paradox of Choice*, argues that an excess of options can lead not to greater satisfaction but to paralysis, anxiety, and a pervasive sense of regret. When faced with thirty varieties of jam on a supermarket shelf, the consumer is not liberated but overwhelmed. The cognitive burden of evaluating each option, weighing the trade-offs, and anticipating the potential consequences of making the "wrong" choice can be so daunting that many people either defer the decision altogether or, having made a choice, are plagued by second thoughts and a nagging suspicion that a better option was available.

This phenomenon extends far beyond the realm of consumer goods. In the professional sphere, employees who are presented with an array of career paths, training opportunities, and lateral moves often find themselves mired in indecision. Rather than viewing these options as empowering, they experience what psychologists call "choice overload" — a state in which the sheer volume of alternatives renders the act of choosing not merely difficult but actively distressing. The fear of making a suboptimal decision can be so acute that it inhibits action entirely, leading to a kind of professional stagnation that is paradoxically born of abundance rather than scarcity.

The digital age has exacerbated this problem in myriad ways. Social media, for instance, provides a constant stream of curated images and narratives that serve as implicit benchmarks against which individuals measure their own choices. The awareness that friends, colleagues, and strangers have made different — and seemingly better — decisions fuels a phenomenon known as "FOMO" (Fear of Missing Out), which further compounds the anxiety associated with choice. Every decision, from what to have for dinner to which city to live in, is shadowed by the awareness that an infinite number of alternative realities exist, each potentially more fulfilling than the one that was chosen.

There are, however, strategies that can help individuals navigate this landscape of overwhelming choice. Schwartz distinguishes between "maximisers" — those who compulsively seek the best possible option — and "satisficers" — those who set a threshold of acceptability and choose the first option that meets it. Research consistently shows that satisficers tend to be happier and less stressed than maximisers, despite the fact that their choices may be objectively less optimal. The lesson here is both counterintuitive and profoundly liberating: in a world of infinite options, the pursuit of "good enough" may be a wiser strategy than the relentless pursuit of "the best."

Furthermore, experts recommend deliberately limiting one's options as a means of reclaiming agency. By establishing clear criteria in advance, restricting the number of alternatives considered, and accepting that no decision is ever truly irreversible, individuals can reduce the cognitive load associated with choice and restore a sense of control. In an age where more is almost always assumed to be better, the deliberate cultivation of less — fewer options, fewer comparisons, fewer regrets — may well be the key to a more contented and purposeful life.

### Glossário

| Palavra/Expressão | Significado em Português |
|---|---|
| conceivable | concebível, imaginável |
| unequivocal | inequívoco, sem ambiguidade |
| unadulterated | puro, sem adulteração |
| pervasive | difundido, penetrante |
| to defer | adiar, postergar |
| plagued by | atormentado por |
| nagging | persistente, incômodo |
| mired in | preso em, atolado em |
| suboptimal | abaixo do ideal |
| to exacerbate | exacerbar, agravar |
| myriad | inúmeros |
| to compound | agravar, intensificar |
| counterintuitive | contraintuitivo |
| relentless | implacável, incessante |
| to reclaim | reconquistar, reaver |

### Perguntas de Interpretação

1. According to the text, why does an abundance of choice not necessarily lead to greater satisfaction?

2. What is "choice overload" and how does it manifest in the professional sphere?

3. In what way does social media contribute to the anxiety associated with decision-making?

4. What is the difference between "maximisers" and "satisficers", and which group tends to be happier according to the research cited?

5. What strategies does the text recommend for dealing with the paradox of choice?

### Gabarito

1. An abundance of choice can lead to paralysis, anxiety, and regret rather than satisfaction. When faced with too many options, the cognitive burden of evaluating each one and the fear of making the "wrong" choice can be overwhelming, leading people either to defer decisions or to feel perpetual dissatisfaction with the choices they do make.

2. Choice overload is a state in which the sheer volume of alternatives makes the act of choosing actively distressing rather than empowering. In the professional sphere, employees presented with numerous career paths and opportunities may become paralysed by indecision, resulting in professional stagnation born of abundance rather than scarcity.

3. Social media provides a constant stream of curated images and narratives that serve as implicit benchmarks. The awareness that others have made different and seemingly better choices fuels FOMO (Fear of Missing Out), which compounds the anxiety associated with every decision.

4. Maximisers compulsively seek the best possible option, while satisficers set a threshold of acceptability and choose the first option that meets it. Research consistently shows that satisficers tend to be happier and less stressed, even though their choices may be objectively less optimal.

5. The text recommends adopting a "satisficing" approach (pursuing "good enough" rather than "the best"), deliberately limiting one's options, establishing clear criteria in advance, restricting the number of alternatives considered, and accepting that no decision is truly irreversible.

---

reading.title: Texto 2: The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence in Criminal Justice
reading.content:
The integration of artificial intelligence into the criminal justice system has been hailed by proponents as a watershed moment in the quest for objectivity, efficiency, and consistency in legal decision-making. Algorithms are now being deployed at virtually every stage of the judicial process — from predictive policing, which uses historical data to forecast where crimes are likely to occur, to risk assessment tools that inform judges' decisions on bail, sentencing, and parole. The appeal of these technologies is readily apparent: unlike human judges, algorithms do not grow weary, harbour personal grudges, or succumb to the cognitive biases that have been shown to influence judicial outcomes. A study by Danziger, Levav, and Avnaim-Pesso famously demonstrated that judges were significantly more likely to grant parole immediately after a meal break — a finding that starkly illustrates the extent to which human decision-making can be swayed by factors that have nothing whatsoever to do with the merits of the case at hand.

However, the notion that algorithms are inherently objective is a dangerous misconception. Machine learning systems are trained on historical data, and if that data reflects the biases, inequalities, and discriminatory practices of the past, the algorithm will inevitably reproduce and even amplify those patterns. In the United States, the COMPAS (Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alternative Sanctions) algorithm, which is used in several states to assess the likelihood of a defendant reoffending, has been shown to disproportionately flag Black defendants as high-risk compared to their white counterparts, even when controlling for relevant variables such as prior criminal history. The algorithm does not explicitly use race as an input variable, but because race is correlated with numerous other factors that the algorithm does consider — such as neighbourhood, employment history, and educational attainment — the resulting predictions are imbued with a racial bias that is all the more insidious for being hidden behind a veneer of mathematical objectivity.

This raises profound questions about accountability and transparency. When a human judge makes a biased decision, there are mechanisms — however imperfect — for identifying, challenging, and rectifying that bias. The decision can be appealed, the judge's reasoning can be scrutinised, and patterns of discrimination can be identified through statistical analysis. But when an algorithm produces a biased outcome, the process by which it arrived at that outcome is often opaque, even to its creators. Many of the most powerful machine learning models are so-called "black boxes" — systems whose internal logic is too complex to be meaningfully interpreted by human beings. This opacity undermines the fundamental legal principle that individuals should be able to understand and challenge the basis on which decisions affecting their liberty are made.

Critics further argue that the deployment of AI in criminal justice risks entrenching a deterministic view of human behaviour. By reducing individuals to a set of data points and statistical probabilities, these systems implicitly deny the capacity for change, growth, and rehabilitation that lies at the heart of any humane approach to justice. A defendant who is classified as "high-risk" by an algorithm is effectively being judged not on the basis of what they have done, but on the basis of what a statistical model predicts they might do — a form of pre-crime that sits uncomfortably with the presumption of innocence.

None of this is to suggest that AI has no role to play in the pursuit of justice. Used thoughtfully and transparently, these tools have the potential to reduce certain forms of bias, improve the consistency of decision-making, and free up judicial resources. But their deployment must be accompanied by robust safeguards: rigorous auditing for bias, meaningful transparency about how the algorithms work and what data they use, the right to challenge algorithmic decisions, and an unwavering commitment to the principle that technology should serve justice, not supplant it. The risk is not that AI will make the criminal justice system perfect; it is that the illusion of objectivity will make us complacent about its imperfections.

### Glossário

| Palavra/Expressão | Significado em Português |
|---|---|
| watershed moment | momento divisor de águas |
| to deploy | implantar, implementar |
| to harbour | abrigar, nutrir (sentimentos) |
| to succumb to | sucumbir a |
| to sway | influenciar, balançar |
| inherently | inerentemente |
| to amplify | amplificar |
| counterparts | contrapartes, equivalentes |
| imbued with | imbuído de, impregnado de |
| insidious | insidioso, traiçoeiro |
| veneer | verniz, camada superficial |
| to rectify | retificar, corrigir |
| to scrutinise | escrutinar, examinar minuciosamente |
| opaque | opaco, não transparente |
| to entrench | entrincheirar, consolidar |
| deterministic | determinístico |
| to supplant | suplantar, substituir |
| complacent | complacente, autoindulgente |
| safeguards | salvaguardas |
| unwavering | inabalável |

### Perguntas de Interpretação

1. Why do proponents argue that AI could improve the criminal justice system?

2. What fundamental flaw does the text identify in the claim that algorithms are objective?

3. How does the COMPAS algorithm illustrate the problem of algorithmic bias?

4. Why does the text argue that algorithmic decision-making undermines the legal principle of transparency?

5. What safeguards does the author recommend to ensure the responsible use of AI in criminal justice?

### Gabarito

1. Proponents argue that unlike human judges, algorithms do not grow weary, harbour personal grudges, or succumb to cognitive biases. They point to research showing that human judicial decisions can be influenced by irrelevant factors (such as whether a judge has recently eaten), suggesting that AI could provide greater objectivity, efficiency, and consistency.

2. The fundamental flaw is that machine learning systems are trained on historical data, and if that data reflects past biases, inequalities, and discriminatory practices, the algorithm will reproduce and amplify those patterns. The objectivity is therefore illusory.

3. COMPAS has been shown to disproportionately flag Black defendants as high-risk compared to white counterparts, even when controlling for prior criminal history. Although race is not an explicit input variable, it is correlated with other factors the algorithm does consider (neighbourhood, employment, education), resulting in racially biased predictions hidden behind apparent mathematical objectivity.

4. Many powerful machine learning models are "black boxes" whose internal logic is too complex to be meaningfully interpreted by humans. This opacity means individuals cannot understand or challenge the basis on which decisions affecting their liberty are made, undermining a fundamental legal principle.

5. The author recommends rigorous auditing for bias, meaningful transparency about how algorithms work and what data they use, the right to challenge algorithmic decisions, and an unwavering commitment to the principle that technology should serve justice rather than replace it.

---

reading.title: Texto 3: The Transformation of Work in the Post-Pandemic World
reading.content:
The global pandemic that swept across the world in 2020 functioned as an involuntary experiment on an unprecedented scale — an experiment that upended long-held assumptions about the nature, location, and organisation of work. Within the space of weeks, millions of employees who had previously commuted to offices five days a week found themselves working from kitchen tables, spare bedrooms, and hastily assembled home offices. Meetings that had been conducted in person were transferred to video conferencing platforms. Business trips were replaced by virtual calls. The daily rituals of office life — the morning coffee in the communal kitchen, the spontaneous conversations in corridors, the after-work drinks on a Friday — evaporated almost overnight.

What was initially conceived as a temporary measure has, in many cases, crystallised into a permanent transformation. Surveys consistently indicate that a significant majority of knowledge workers do not wish to return to the office full-time. Having experienced the benefits of remote work — the elimination of exhausting commutes, the greater flexibility in managing personal and professional responsibilities, the ability to work in environments tailored to individual preferences — many employees are now pushing back against employers who attempt to enforce a blanket return to the office. The result has been the emergence of the so-called "hybrid" model, in which employees split their time between the office and remote locations, typically spending two or three days per week on-site.

For employers, this shift presents both opportunities and challenges. On the one hand, the adoption of remote and hybrid working has expanded the talent pool dramatically. Companies that were previously constrained by geography — forced to recruit from the immediate vicinity of their physical offices — can now tap into a global workforce. This has been particularly transformative for organisations based in expensive metropolitan areas, which can now offer competitive salaries to candidates in regions with lower costs of living, achieving savings on both recruitment and real estate. On the other hand, managers have been confronted with the challenge of maintaining team cohesion, fostering a sense of belonging, and preserving the informal knowledge transfer and spontaneous collaboration that physical proximity facilitates.

The shift has also laid bare deep inequalities in the workforce. The luxury of working from home is largely confined to a privileged subset of the labour market — white-collar professionals in sectors such as technology, finance, and consulting. For the millions of workers in sectors such as hospitality, retail, manufacturing, healthcare, and logistics, the notion of remote work is simply not applicable. These workers — often among the lowest paid and least protected — were the ones who continued to show up in person throughout the pandemic, frequently at considerable risk to their own health. The celebration of remote work as a universal benefit rings hollow when viewed against this backdrop of structural inequality.

Looking ahead, the long-term consequences of this transformation remain uncertain. Some commentators predict that the decline of the traditional office will lead to the hollowing out of city centres, as the restaurants, shops, and services that depend on commuter footfall struggle to survive. Others argue that cities will adapt, as they have always done, reinventing themselves as hubs for culture, collaboration, and community rather than merely sites of obligatory daily attendance. What is clear, however, is that the relationship between work and place has been irrevocably altered. The genie, as it were, is out of the bottle: having proven that productivity can be maintained — and in many cases improved — outside the confines of the traditional office, neither employers nor employees are likely to return to the old model without a fight.

The most profound shift may ultimately be not logistical but psychological. The pandemic forced millions of people to confront fundamental questions about the role of work in their lives — questions that many had previously been too busy, or too comfortable, to ask. What do I actually need from a workplace? Is the career trajectory I have been following aligned with my values and priorities? Am I willing to sacrifice my well-being for a pay cheque? The answers to these questions, arrived at in the solitude of locked-down homes around the world, are reshaping not only how we work but why we work, and what we expect from the institutions that employ us.

### Glossário

| Palavra/Expressão | Significado em Português |
|---|---|
| to upend | virar de cabeça para baixo, subverter |
| hastily | apressadamente |
| to crystallise | cristalizar-se, tomar forma definitiva |
| to push back | resistir, opor-se |
| blanket (adj.) | generalizado, abrangente |
| to tap into | aproveitar, acessar |
| to lay bare | revelar, expor |
| to ring hollow | soar falso, não convencer |
| backdrop | pano de fundo |
| footfall | fluxo de pedestres / clientes |
| to hollow out | esvaziar por dentro |
| irrevocably | irrevogavelmente |
| the genie is out of the bottle | não tem volta (expressão idiomática) |
| confines | limites, fronteiras |
| trajectory | trajetória |
| solitude | solidão |

### Perguntas de Interpretação

1. How did the pandemic function as an "involuntary experiment" according to the text?

2. Why are many employees resistant to returning to the office full-time?

3. What advantages and challenges does the hybrid model present for employers?

4. Why does the text argue that the celebration of remote work as a universal benefit is problematic?

5. What does the author suggest may be the most profound long-term consequence of the pandemic's impact on work?

### Gabarito

1. The pandemic forced millions of employees who had previously commuted to offices to suddenly work from home, effectively testing on an unprecedented scale whether work could be effectively performed outside traditional office settings, without any prior planning or voluntary participation.

2. Having experienced the benefits of remote work — such as the elimination of exhausting commutes, greater flexibility in managing personal and professional responsibilities, and the ability to work in environments tailored to individual preferences — many employees do not wish to give up these advantages.

3. Advantages include an expanded talent pool (companies can recruit globally rather than being constrained by geography) and savings on recruitment and real estate. Challenges include maintaining team cohesion, fostering a sense of belonging, and preserving the informal knowledge transfer and spontaneous collaboration that physical proximity facilitates.

4. The ability to work from home is largely confined to white-collar professionals in sectors like technology, finance, and consulting. Workers in hospitality, retail, manufacturing, healthcare, and logistics — often the lowest paid and least protected — cannot work remotely and continued to work in person at considerable risk during the pandemic. Celebrating remote work as universally beneficial ignores this structural inequality.

5. The author suggests the most profound shift may be psychological rather than logistical. The pandemic forced people to confront fundamental questions about the role of work in their lives, their values, priorities, and well-being — questions whose answers are reshaping not only how we work but why we work and what we expect from employers.

communication.title: Diálogo 1: Negociação Salarial — Reunião com o Gestor
communication.content:
**Contexto:** Sarah é uma analista de dados sênior que trabalha na empresa há três anos. Ela marcou uma reunião com seu gerente, David, para discutir uma revisão salarial.

---

**Sarah:** David, thank you for making time for this meeting. I know how busy things have been with the end-of-quarter reports.

**David:** Of course, Sarah. You mentioned you wanted to discuss your remuneration package. I'm all ears.

**Sarah:** Yes, that's right. I've been with the company for three years now, and during that time I've taken on considerably more responsibility than my original role entailed. When I was hired, I was primarily responsible for data cleaning and basic reporting. Since then, I've been leading the development of our predictive analytics platform, mentoring two junior analysts, and I was the one who spearheaded the migration to the new data warehouse last year.

**David:** I won't dispute any of that. Your contribution to the data warehouse migration in particular was exceptional. The project came in on time and under budget, which is practically unheard of in this company.

**Sarah:** I appreciate you saying that. And it's precisely because of this expanded scope that I'd like to discuss an adjustment to my salary. I've done some research — looking at market rates for senior data analysts with my level of experience and qualifications in this region — and I've found that my current compensation is approximately fifteen per cent below the median. I'm not asking for anything outrageous — I simply think my salary should reflect the value I bring to the team and be in line with what the market is paying.

**David:** I hear you, and I want to be transparent with you. Budgets have been tight this year — you know as well as I do that the macroeconomic environment has been challenging. That said, I recognise that retaining top talent is a priority, and I don't want you to feel undervalued.

**Sarah:** I understand the constraints, David, and I'm not trying to put you in a difficult position. But I think it's important to consider the cost of not addressing this. If I were to leave, the company would need to recruit and train a replacement, which, as you know, is far more expensive than adjusting the compensation of someone who is already performing at a high level.

**David:** That's a fair point. Let me be straight with you. I don't have the authority to approve a fifteen per cent increase unilaterally — anything above ten per cent needs to go through the Compensation Committee. What I can do is put together a strong business case on your behalf and advocate for a meaningful adjustment. Would you be open to a phased approach — say, a ten per cent increase now, with a further review in six months?

**Sarah:** I appreciate that, David, and I'm open to discussing a phased approach. However, I'd want some assurance that the six-month review would be conducted against clear, predefined criteria — not just a vague promise to "revisit" the issue.

**David:** Absolutely. We can document specific performance metrics and milestones that would trigger the second phase of the adjustment. I'll have HR draw up a development plan that makes the criteria explicit. Does that sound reasonable?

**Sarah:** It does. I'd also like to discuss whether there's scope for non-salary benefits — for instance, additional professional development funding or an increase in my annual leave allocation. These things matter to me almost as much as the salary itself.

**David:** I think there's definitely room to explore that. The professional development budget is separate from the salary budget, so that may actually be easier to approve. Let me look into both and come back to you by the end of the week with a concrete proposal.

**Sarah:** That would be great. I want you to know that I'm committed to this company and to this team. This isn't an ultimatum — it's a conversation about making sure that commitment is mutual.

**David:** I understand, and I respect the way you've approached this. You've made a compelling case, and I'll do my best to ensure the outcome reflects your contribution. Let's touch base on Friday.

**Sarah:** Perfect. Thank you, David.

---

communication.title: Diálogo 2: Reunião de Equipe — Discussão sobre Estratégia de Lançamento de Produto
communication.content:
**Contexto:** Uma equipe multidisciplinar está reunida para discutir a estratégia de lançamento de um novo produto de software. Participam: Elena (Product Manager), James (Head of Marketing), Priya (Lead Developer) e Marcus (Finance Director).

---

**Elena:** Right, thank you all for coming. As you know, we're looking at a Q3 launch for the new analytics dashboard. The purpose of this meeting is to align on the go-to-market strategy and identify any outstanding risks. James, can you walk us through the marketing plan?

**James:** Sure. So, our current thinking is to take a phased approach to the launch. Phase one would be a soft launch — invite-only beta access for our existing enterprise clients. This serves two purposes: it gives us real-world feedback from power users, and it creates a sense of exclusivity that we can leverage in our broader marketing campaign. Phase two, roughly four weeks later, would be the public launch, accompanied by a content blitz — thought leadership articles, webinars, case studies based on the beta feedback, and a targeted social media campaign.

**Elena:** That sounds solid. Priya, from a development standpoint, are we on track for a Q3 delivery?

**Priya:** We're on track, but with some caveats. The core functionality is ready and has been through two rounds of QA. However, the advanced customisation features that James's team has been promising in the marketing materials are not fully built out yet. If we push those into the beta, we risk delivering a half-baked experience to our most important clients. My recommendation would be to launch the beta with the core features only and roll out the advanced customisation in a subsequent update.

**James:** I understand the technical constraints, but from a marketing perspective, the customisation features are our key differentiator. They're what sets us apart from the competition. If we launch without them, even in beta, we risk underwhelming our audience and losing the narrative.

**Priya:** I appreciate that, James, but I'd rather underwhelm slightly than have the product crash or behave unpredictably in front of our biggest clients. The reputational damage of a buggy launch would far outweigh any short-term marketing gains.

**Marcus:** If I may weigh in from a financial perspective — Priya raises a valid point about reputational risk, but James is right that the customisation features are central to our value proposition. The pricing model we've developed is predicated on the assumption that enterprise clients will pay a premium for those features. If we launch without them, we may need to revisit the pricing strategy, which could have significant revenue implications.

**Elena:** This is exactly why we're having this conversation. Let me suggest a compromise. Priya, what if we included the customisation features in the beta but limited access to a smaller subset of clients — say, five or six of our most trusted partners — and positioned it explicitly as an early preview? That way, James gets to showcase the differentiator, Marcus can validate the pricing assumptions, and we contain the reputational risk.

**Priya:** I could live with that, provided we're transparent with those clients that the features are in preview and may have some rough edges. I'd also want to assign a dedicated support engineer to each of those accounts during the beta period to handle any issues in real time.

**James:** That actually works from a marketing angle too. We can frame it as an "exclusive early access" programme, which feeds into the narrative of exclusivity I was talking about.

**Marcus:** And from a financial standpoint, a smaller beta gives us enough data to validate the pricing model without committing to the premium tier until the features are fully polished. I'm comfortable with that approach.

**Elena:** Excellent. So, to summarise: we'll proceed with a soft launch in Q3. The beta will include core features for all invited clients, plus early-preview access to the customisation features for a select group of five to six trusted partners. Priya will assign dedicated support engineers, and James will position the early access as an exclusive programme. Marcus, I'll need updated revenue projections based on this revised timeline by next Wednesday. Does anyone have any objections?

**James:** None from me.

**Priya:** I'm on board.

**Marcus:** Agreed.

**Elena:** Great. Let's reconvene in two weeks to review progress. Thank you, everyone.

---

communication.title: Diálogo 3: Entrevista de Emprego — Cargo de Gerente de Projetos Sênior
communication.content:
**Contexto:** Anna está sendo entrevistada para o cargo de Senior Project Manager em uma grande consultoria internacional. A entrevistadora é Ms. Catherine Hayes, Diretora de Operações.

---

**Catherine:** Good morning, Anna. Thank you for coming in today. I've reviewed your CV and I must say, your experience is impressive. Before we get into the specifics, could you tell me a little about what attracted you to this role?

**Anna:** Good morning, Catherine. Thank you for the opportunity. What attracted me, first and foremost, was the scope and complexity of the projects your firm manages. In my current role, I oversee a portfolio of projects valued at approximately twenty million pounds, but they tend to be domestic in scope. I'm at a point in my career where I'm looking for the challenge of managing cross-border projects with multiple stakeholders, diverse regulatory environments, and the cultural complexity that comes with operating across different markets. Your firm's reputation for delivering large-scale transformation programmes in precisely these contexts is what drew me to the position.

**Catherine:** That's good to hear. Let me ask you this — can you describe a situation in which a project you were managing encountered a significant setback, and how you handled it?

**Anna:** Certainly. About eighteen months ago, I was leading a large-scale ERP implementation for a financial services client. We were roughly six months into the project when the client's newly appointed CTO decided to fundamentally change the scope — shifting from a phased rollout to a big-bang implementation across all twenty-three of their regional offices simultaneously. This effectively doubled the workload without increasing the budget or the timeline.

**Catherine:** That sounds incredibly challenging. How did you respond?

**Anna:** My first instinct was to push back, but I realised that the CTO was under enormous pressure from the board to demonstrate rapid transformation. Simply saying "no" would have damaged the relationship and potentially cost us the contract. Instead, I requested a forty-eight-hour window to conduct a thorough impact assessment. I worked with my technical leads to quantify the additional resources, costs, and risks associated with the big-bang approach and presented the CTO with three options: the original phased plan, the full big-bang approach with a revised budget and timeline, and a hybrid option that would implement the core modules simultaneously but phase in the more complex integrations.

**Catherine:** And which option did the CTO choose?

**Anna:** He went with the hybrid approach, which was the one I had designed to be the most attractive. It gave him the optics of a company-wide simultaneous launch — which was what he needed to present to the board — while managing the technical risk. We delivered the core modules on time and within a five per cent variance on budget, and the phased integrations were completed over the following quarter.

**Catherine:** Excellent. That demonstrates a very mature approach to stakeholder management. Let me shift gears slightly. How would you describe your leadership style?

**Anna:** I would describe myself as a facilitative leader. I believe very strongly that the project manager's role is not to have all the answers but to create the conditions in which the team can do their best work. That means being clear about expectations and priorities, removing obstacles, and creating psychological safety so that people feel comfortable raising concerns early rather than burying problems until they escalate. I'm also a firm believer in structured retrospectives — I make sure that every project milestone is followed by a thorough review of what went well, what didn't, and what we would do differently next time.

**Catherine:** That resonates with our culture here. One final question — where do you see yourself in five years?

**Anna:** Honestly, I try not to be too prescriptive about long-term career plans, because I've learned that the most interesting opportunities are often the ones you don't anticipate. What I can say is that I want to be in a role where I'm operating at the intersection of strategy and execution — not just delivering projects but shaping the methodology and building the capability of the teams around me. If this role allows me to grow in that direction, I would consider that a success.

**Catherine:** That's a refreshingly honest answer. Anna, I've enjoyed this conversation. We'll be making our decision by the end of next week and will be in touch. Do you have any questions for me?

**Anna:** Just one. What would you say is the single biggest challenge facing the project management function in your organisation right now?

**Catherine:** That's a great question. Honestly, it's capacity. We're winning more work than we can comfortably deliver, and we need experienced people who can hit the ground running and build out the team around them. That's exactly why this role exists.

**Anna:** That's encouraging to hear. Thank you for your time, Catherine.

**Catherine:** Thank you, Anna. We'll be in touch.

---

exercises.title: Exercício 1 — Mixed Conditionals
exercises.content:
1. If she **had studied** law, she **would be** a solicitor now.
2. If I **were** more patient, I **would not have lost** my temper yesterday.
3. If they **had invested** in property ten years ago, they **would be** wealthy today.
4. If he **spoke** better English, he **would have got** the job in London last year.

exercises.title: Exercício 2 — Conditional Inversion
exercises.content:
5. **Had I known** about the problem, I would have acted sooner.
6. **Were she to resign**, the company would be in trouble.
7. **Should you have** any questions, please contact the office.
8. **Had it not been** for his quick thinking, the accident would have been much worse.

exercises.title: Exercício 3 — Cleft Sentences
exercises.content:
9. **It was John who** broke the window.
10. **What I need is** a holiday.
11. **What annoys me most is** her attitude.
12. **It was the lack of communication that** caused the project to fail.

exercises.title: Exercício 4 — Subjunctive
exercises.content:
13. The manager insisted that every employee **attend** the training.
14. It is essential that the document **be submitted** before the deadline.
15. The committee recommended that the policy **be reviewed**.
16. It is imperative that he **be** informed immediately.

exercises.title: Exercício 5 — Passive with Reporting Verbs
exercises.content:
17. → It **is believed that the company is planning a merger**.
    → The company **is believed to be planning a merger**.

18. → It **is said that the new treatment is effective**.
    → The new treatment **is said to be effective**.

exercises.title: Exercício 6 — Participle Clauses
exercises.content:
19. **Having finished the presentation**, she sent it to her manager.
20. **Built in 1850**, the bridge is still in use today.
21. **Not knowing the answer**, he remained silent.
22. **Having been warned about the weather**, we decided to cancel the trip.

exercises.title: Exercício 7 — Discourse Markers
exercises.content:
23. **Much as** I appreciate your effort, I cannot approve the proposal in its current form.
24. She can barely use a spreadsheet, **let alone** design a database.
25. The results were encouraging, **albeit** somewhat inconclusive.
26. **Given that** the budget constraints, we should consider a more modest approach.
    *(Nota: "Given the budget constraints" — sem "that" — também é aceito e mais natural neste contexto.)*
27. He completed the certification, **thereby** qualifying for the promotion.
28. The objections **notwithstanding**, the project was approved by the board.

exercises.title: Exercício 8 — Fronting and Inversion
exercises.content:
29. **Never have I seen** such incompetence.
30. **Hardly had she finished** speaking when the audience erupted in applause.
31. **Only after the report had been published did they realise** the error.
32. **Not only did he break** the record, **but he also won** a gold medal.

exercises.title: Exercício 9 — Phrasal Verbs
exercises.content:
33. The police **cracked down on** illegal parking in the city centre.
34. She was **singled out** for praise by the managing director.
35. The deal **fell through** because the two sides couldn't agree on the price.
36. The government **brought about** significant changes to the healthcare system.
37. He **turned down** the job offer because it required relocation.
38. A thorough investigation was **carried out** by the internal audit team.
39. The old technology is being **phased out** in favour of more modern solutions.
40. The police have not **ruled out** the possibility of foul play.

exercises.title: Exercício 10 — Collocations
exercises.content:
41. **compelling** proof
42. far-**reaching** consequences
43. **raise** awareness
44. take **precedence**
45. **mounting** pressure

exercises.title: Exercício 11 — Tradução
exercises.content:
46. **Were it not for her dedication, the project would have failed.** *(Ou: Had it not been for her dedication...)*
47. **It is widely believed that the company will be sold.**
48. **All I want is some peace and quiet.**
49. **She needn't have worried — everything turned out fine.**
50. **Not only did he fail the exam, but he also lost his scholarship.**
51. **It is high time the government addressed the housing crisis.**
52. **What surprised me was how calm she remained under pressure.**

exercises.title: Exercício 12 — Wish / Would rather / It's time
exercises.content:
53. I wish I **knew** how to play the piano.
54. If only she **hadn't resigned** so hastily.
55. I'd rather you **didn't mention** this to anyone.
56. It's high time the company **invested** in new technology.
57. She wishes he **were / was** more considerate.

exercises.title: Exercício 14 — Error Correction
exercises.content:
63. ~~If I would have known~~ → **If I had known**, I would have told you.
64. ~~It is essential that every student attends~~ → It is essential that every student **attend** the orientation. *(Subjuntivo: forma base, sem "s")*
65. ~~Hardly she had arrived~~ → **Hardly had she arrived** when the meeting started. *(Inversão obrigatória após "hardly")*
66. ~~The suspect is alleged to fled~~ → The suspect is alleged **to have fled** the country. *(to + have + past participle)*
67. ~~He denied to have~~ → He denied **having** any involvement in the scandal. *(deny + -ing)*

---

*Fim do livro didático C1 — Advanced English.*
